history:
On board their small boat, $Milius$FRPierre_Bernard_Milius$ and Faure discovered that â€œto the east, at the bottom of the basin [Western Port], an island was connected to the mainland by a mud bank.â€ From the other boat accompanying the surveying mission, BrÃ©vedent circumnavigated it, and the island was named ÃŽle des FranÃ§ois [French Island]. In his journal, the botanist Leschenault, who was accompanying $Milius$FRPierre_Bernard_Milius$, noted the following about the island: "On the 20th of Germinal, Year X [10 April 1802], we landed in the evening on the
western side of the second channel. Lieutenant-commander $Milius$FRPierre_Bernard_Milius$ perceived that this portion
of land, which is marked on the English chart as forming part of the mainland, is in fact an
island. It appears to be inhabited only occasionally, as we came across just a few, very old,
traces of fire, and the vegetation had already recovered from the ravages of the flames â€“ with
all the vigour that occurs where an excellent soil is kept damp by a perpetual cover of shade.
When we looked for a place to spend the night, it was only with difficulty that we found a
small area that was not entirely clogged with bushes and creepers. It was a rather unpleasant
night, as we were attacked by swarms of mosquitoes. We slept around a large fire, which kept
them at bay to some extent."

history:

history: According to a well-known legend, after the Battle of Marengo, Bonaparteâ€”famished after the fightingâ€”asked his cook, FranÃ§ois Claude Guignet, known as Dunand, to prepare him a meal. In subsequent versions, chicken was sometimes replaced by veal, considered a more refined meat.

$PÃ©ron$FranÃ§ois_PÃ©ron$ extends the notion of the coastâ€™s inhospitality to include its animal life. history:
It is said (but not proven) that NapolÃ©on discovered a pair of special points (the NapolÃ©on points) at the center of a triangle, which are interconnected with the Fermat pointâ€”the point whose total distance from the three vertices of the triangle is minimized.
Fermat made other major mathematical contributions and is best remembered for his last theorem that states that no three positive integers a, b, and c satisfy the equation an+ bn = cn for any integer value of n greater than 2. For over three centuries, mathematicians struggled with its proof, which Fermat famously said he could not fit into the narrow margin of the page he was working on.
Remaining a conjecture for almost 300 years, Fermat's Last Theorem was finally proven in 1993 by Andrew Wiles, using a complex proof that likely relied on mathematical approaches and tools very different from those available to Fermat. An international collaborative project is now working to "teach" this proof to a computer.

On the Isle de France, $Decaen$Charles_Mathieu_Isidore_Decaen$ organized the system of national education and introduced an adapted version of the Napoleonic Civil Code, known as the Code $Decaen$Charles_Mathieu_Isidore_Decaen$. $Decaen$Charles_Mathieu_Isidore_Decaen$ became the personal adversary of $Matthew Flinders$Matthew_Flinders$, whom he kept under house arrest on the island from December 1803 to June 1810. Although the British Admiralty held an unfavorable opinion of $Decaen$Charles_Mathieu_Isidore_Decaen$, Napoleon Bonaparte regarded him as a man of moderation. In response to $Decaen$Charles_Mathieu_Isidore_Decaen$â€™s defense of General Moreauâ€”who had been implicated in contacts with conspirators against the Emperorâ€”Napoleon is reported to have remarked: â€œYou are kind, and you think that everybody is like you.â€

The Knob, named  Cap Mably by the French, stands at the edge of Franklin Harbor, a broad sheltered inlet. In earlier times, this was a more open bay, but over a long period sand and shingle gradually built out from the shore, narrowing its entrance. The headland preserves a series of old beach ridges, formed by storm waves, which show how the coastline slowly advanced and changed shape through time. These features make the site an important witness to the coastal history of the harbour. 
It was named by the French Cap Mably, after Gabriel Bonnot de Mably (1709  -1785),  a writer and philosopher of the Enlightenment, brother of Condillac. (see Cap Condillac).

history:
Freycinet gives a favourable description of ÃŽle Lagrange, which he surveyed aboard the Casuarina. He particularly emphasises the fertile appearance of this land, in contrast with the more arid coasts observed earlier:
ÃŽle Lagrange is remarkable in its formation; although it is not very high, it nevertheless contains hills, all of which presented to us an appearance of fertility to which our eyes had long been unaccustomed. The south-western point of this island, near which a peak may be seen, is low and rocky, and forms a natural quay so steep-to that the largest ships could come alongside it. At a distance of two cable-lengths, the sounding line constantly gave us 11 fathoms. Several deep coves lie on the southern part of the island; their shores are bordered with pebbles.â€
This description presents an island that appeared both welcoming and useful for navigation, suggesting the potential interest that the island and harbour may have held as a possible stopping place on maritime routes, particularly those leading towards Asia.

PÃ©ron choisit de nommer cap Rivoli un promontoire situÃ© sur lâ€™Ã®le Marengo, associant ainsi deux grandes victoires italiennes de Bonaparte : Rivoli et Marengo. history:
PÃ©ron chose to name a promontory on Marengo Island Cape Rivoli, thereby bringing together two major Italian victories associated with Bonaparte: Rivoli and Marengo. This toponymic association was not accidental. It inscribed both the island and the cape within the same Napoleonic memory, that of the Italian campaigns which helped establish Bonaparte as a leading military and political figure.
The victory of Rivoli, in 1797, played a decisive role in consolidating his military glory under the Directory. Marengo, won in 1800 after the coup of 18 Brumaire, confirmed his authority as First Consul. The names of these two victories therefore naturally belonged in the gulf dedicated to Bonaparte, Bonaparte Gulf. By bringing them together within the same geographical space, PÃ©ron gave the southern landscape a powerful commemorative charge: nomenclature became a narrative of Bonaparteâ€™s rise, from victorious general of the Italian campaign to head of the new Consular regime.

characteristic:
Spencer Gulf, in South Australia, is a vast, shallow, triangular marine depression situated between Eyre Peninsula to the west and Yorke Peninsula to the east. Its botanical and zoological characteristics are closely linked to its geomorphological forms and hydrology. Its shallow, semi-enclosed basin, intertidal mudflats, sandy shoals, low and sheltered shores, and rocky outcrops determine the distribution of its principal coastal and marine habitats.
These forms are complemented by a distinctive hydrology, marked by limited water renewal, strong evaporation, restricted freshwater input, and increasing salinity towards the northern end of the gulf. This combination favours the development of seagrass meadows, mangroves, salt marshes, and benthic communities adapted to warm, saline, and relatively confined waters. It also helps make Spencer Gulf a nursery area for many fish and crustaceans, as well as an important habitat for various species of coastal birds and for the Australian giant cuttlefish.
Matthew Flinders, who was the first European to explore the gulf in March 1802, named it after George John Spencer, 2nd Earl Spencer (1758â€“1834), who was First Lord of the Admiralty when Flinders was preparing his voyage to Terra Australis. The French expedition reached the gulf after Flinders, in April 1802, and charted its coastline during a second visit the following year. PÃ©ron named it Golfe Bonaparte, but in later years it reverted to the name chosen by its first European explorer.

history:
PÃ©ron emphasises the remarkable scale of this vast gulf, which he describes as â€œpresenting itself in the form of the mouth of a very great riverâ€ and ending, â€œas it gradually narrows towards its head, in sandbanks that obstruct it.â€
He nevertheless stresses the formidable character of these waters. On â€œtwo different occasionsâ€ â€” in April 1802 and again in January 1803 â€” the expedition had to survey these dangerous shores: â€œhurricanes and storms, shoals and breakers always came close to causing our destruction.â€ Twice, on 13 and 19 April 1802 according to PÃ©ron, the *GÃ©ographe* attempted to penetrate to the head of Golfe Bonaparte; twice, the crew found itself, in his own words, â€œon the point of perishing there.â€ Only the *Casuarina* was able to complete the survey eight months later.
Freycinet likewise expressed the disappointment of the *Casuarina*â€™s crew when it became clear that no great river flowed into the head of the gulf. Everything, however, seemed to suggest its existence, as he observed: â€œthis vast and deep bed, these imposing banks, these waters whose course extended far beyond the point to which the eye could reach; the extraordinary prolongation of the gulf, its direction, even its very shape, all contributed to making the illusion complete.â€

characteristic:
ÃŽle St Lambert / Williams Island is a U-shaped rocky island located south of the Eyre Peninsula. About 40 m high and bordered by cliffs, it rests on a basement of pink granite, dark porphyritic granitic gneiss, and dolerite dykes, overlain by a calcarenite platform. Its dry landscape, highly exposed to winds and sea spray, is dominated by low vegetation and rocky outcrops.
Jean FranÃ§ois de Saint-Lambert (1716â€“1803) is best known for his descriptive and moral poetry. In Les Saisons (The Seasons, 1769), his major work, he celebrates nature, rural life, and the cycle of the year, while developing philosophical and moral reflections, particularly on the passage of time and the fleeting nature of all things.

history: The term piton does not refer to an entire mountain massif, but rather to a specific summit, an isolated height, or a particularly prominent landform. Freycinet described Piton Borda as â€œthe most remarkable point of a range of isolated mountains visible in the distance.â€ In other words, the name does not apply to the whole range, but to the summit that stands out most clearly and could serve as a visual landmark from the sea. The name Piton Borda was therefore well suited to this distinctive relief feature, useful for coastal orientation, while also honouring a scientist whose instruments and methods were designed precisely to improve the measurement of angles, bearings, and distances in navigation.

Situated on the north-eastern coast of Kangaroo Island, Bougainville Bay / Nepean Bay forms a broad, sheltered, and shallow coastal indentation, deeply articulated by coves, lagoons, and minor inlets. Its shores are bordered by beaches, coastal vegetation, rocky points, mudflats, and shoals. Its most distinctive physical character probably lies in the combination of calm protected waters, low depths, extensive seagrass beds, and estuarine and lagoonal systems.

Sailing with La Boudeuse and Lâ€™Ã‰toile, he travelled across the Pacific, called at Tahiti, Samoa, and the New Hebrides, and played a major part in establishing French exploration within the oceanic horizon of the eighteenth century.

characteristic:
Cape Cassini and the surrounding terrain, on the northern coast of Kangaroo Island, form a land and soil unit defined by the Soil and Land Program of the Government of South Australia.
It consists of a system of rocky gullies, coastal slopes and ridges, with residual native vegetation of significant conservation value, notably as habitat for the rare glossy black-cockatoo. The coastline around Cape Cassini includes, in places, cliffs and steep coastal slopes.
The Cassini family played a very important role in French astronomy and cartography in the 17th and 18th centuries.

history:
In the Napoleonic period, the legacy of the Cassini family remained essential for officers, military geographers and cartographers: their map of France, based on geodetic triangulation, had established a model of scientific precision. However, the military needs of the Empire also revealed its limitations. In 1808, Napoleon I decided to commission a new map of France, more detailed and better suited to the needs of the army.
In the maritime sphere, the influence of the Cassinis was mainly indirect: their work contributed to the learned culture of astronomy, longitude and geodesy, which formed the background to advances in navigation and coastal surveying.

Yet DecrÃ¨s stood above him institutionally and politically, since he directed the whole naval administration as Minister of the Navy and Colonies, a position he held from 1801 to 1814. This is what Freycinet meant when he described Ganteaume as â€œthe most prominent general officer after the Minister who directs all branches of this part of the public force.â€
Freycinet adds, to further justify his choice, that Ganteaumeâ€™s name had "also" been applied to â€œan island ten leagues in circumferenceâ€ on the north coast of New Holland, around 120Â° longitude. This remark is revealing, but it rested on a mistaken identification: the supposed Ganteaume Island was not an actual island, but a bay. Ganteaumeâ€™s name had therefore already entered the expeditionâ€™s nomenclature, though attached to an uncertain geographical feature.

The town of Streaky Bay lies at its southern end, in the protected area of Blanche Port. The coastal landscape combines low beaches, sandy bottoms, seagrass beds and calm bays, while the more exposed coasts of the district feature cliffs, reefs, rocky platforms and beaches beaten by the swells of the Southern Ocean.
The bay received the name Baie Louis-NapolÃ©on in honour of Louis Bonaparte (1778â€“1846), Napoleon Bonaparteâ€™s younger brother, who was appointed King of Holland by Napoleon in 1806.

Cette remarque correspond assez bien Ã  la configuration actuelle du secteur, Ã  condition dâ€™entendre ces â€œmarÃ©cagesâ€ comme des zones humides littorales basses : vasiÃ¨res, marais salÃ©s, samphires et mangroves des milieux intertidaux abritÃ©s, plutÃ´t que comme des marais dâ€™eau douce. Freycinet ajoute que les nombreuses fumÃ©es aperÃ§ues sur la cÃ´te constituaient pour lui un indice certain de prÃ©sence humaine. Cette constatation doit Ãªtre replacÃ©e dans le contexte du pays Wirangu : Streaky Bay se situe sur les terres traditionnelles des Wirangu, avec des liens rÃ©gionaux avec les peuples voisins Barngarla et Nauo.
history:
At the head of Baie Louis â€” present-day Streaky Bay â€” Freycinet was struck by the extreme flatness of the land, which he interpreted as a possible indication of marshy ground. This observation corresponds fairly well to the present configuration of the area, provided that these â€œmarshesâ€ are understood as low-lying coastal wetlands: mudflats, salt marshes, samphire flats and mangroves in sheltered intertidal environments, rather than freshwater marshes.
Freycinet adds that the numerous fires seen along the coast were, for him, a certain indication of human presence. This observation should be placed within the context of Wirangu Country: Streaky Bay lies on the traditional lands of the Wirangu people, with regional connections to the neighbouring Barngarla and Nauo peoples.

characteristic:
Smoky Bay is a large, shallow bay on the western coast of Eyre Peninsula, in South Australia. Although it opens onto the Southern Ocean, it is relatively sheltered by Eyre Island, its associated sand shoals and, farther offshore, the Franklin Islands. These natural features reduce wave energy and give the bay its calm character, favouring the development of distinctive coastal environments.
Much of the shoreline consists of broad, tide-dominated sandflats, in places up to about 500 metres wide. These areas are exposed at low tide, creating a shifting landscape between land and sea. They are associated with mangroves and seagrass meadows, which play an important ecological role: they provide habitat for many species, stabilise sediments and contribute to the biological richness of the bay.
The French named the bay Baie TrÃ©ville, in honour of Louis-RenÃ©-Madeleine Le Vassor de La Touche-TrÃ©ville, known as Latouche-TrÃ©ville (1745-1804), a French naval officer held in particularly high regard by Napoleon. The English name Smoky Bay was given by Matthew Flinders in 1802, in reference to the smoke from fires lit by the Aboriginal people of the region.

history:
Among the members of the expedition, FranÃ§ois PÃ©ron stands out for the number of place names dedicated to him by Freycinet: six in total, a record in the nomenclature. However, the name Canal PÃ©ron does not refer to any direct discoveries made by PÃ©ron in this area, as he did not take part in Freycinet's mission to the Hunter Islands.
In his logbook dated 6-7 December 1802, Freycinet recorded the circumstances of his anchoring in this strait/channel separating the two main islands of the archipelago: "Seeing that I could not gain windward with the sails that the wind forced me to keep, I decided to anchor in the strait formed by the two largest of the Hunter Islands. At 3 o'clock in the evening, I dropped anchor in the northeast of the long island in 20 fathoms of sandy bottom. Mr Boullanger and I decided not to leave our current anchorage without first determining its position for latitude and longitude observations." On the map in the atlas produced as a result of the expedition, Freycinet precisely locates this anchorage, from where he and Boullanger took bearings of the Hunter Islands. These measurements served as the basis for the triangulation of the archipelago, thus contributing to its scientific cartography.

characteristic:
The passage separates Robbins Island from the Tasmanian mainland. Together with Boullanger Bay, it forms a wetland complex of striking ecological and geomorphological significance, characterised by extensive sandflats, powerful tidal currents, and large numbers of shorebirds feeding on the exposed flats at low tide.
The area supports major flocks of migratory shorebirds, some of which undertake among the longest regular migrations in the world. These birds breed in the Northern Hemisphere and travel south each year to feed in Tasmania. Robbins Passageâ€“Boullanger Bay lies at the southern end of the East Asianâ€“Australasian Flyway, a migration route that extends from the Russian Far East and Alaska southwards through East and Southeast Asia to Australia and New Zealand, encompassing 22 countries.
The passage was named after the *Casuarina*, a schooner placed under Freycinetâ€™s command and sent by Baudin, in December 1802, to carry out the survey of the Hunter Islands.

Ã€ mesure quâ€™il progressait plus avant dans lâ€™enfoncement depuis lâ€™est, les bancs de sable devenaient de plus en plus nombreux, jusquâ€™Ã  intercepter finalement tout passage.

Au sud, la cÃ´te sâ€™Ã©lÃ¨ve en collines rÃ©guliÃ¨res, de hauteur moyenne, qui se prolongent vers lâ€™est jusquâ€™Ã  Circular Head sans prÃ©senter de dÃ©coupures importantes. Sur le plan nautique, lâ€™intÃ©rÃªt du lieu rÃ©side surtout dans son embouchure : malgrÃ© les hauts-fonds qui en compliquent lâ€™accÃ¨s, elle pourrait offrir aux petits bÃ¢timents un mouillage sÃ»r et bien abritÃ©, quelles que soient les directions du vent.
Freycinet, dont le regard demeure dâ€™abord tournÃ© vers la gÃ©ographie nautique du lieu, relÃ¨ve toutefois aussi la richesse de lâ€™avifaune. Aux abords des Ã®les Hunter, il signale la prÃ©sence considÃ©rable de pÃ©trels et de cormorans sur les Ã®lots voisins. Dans lâ€™entrÃ©e du Casuarina elle-mÃªme, il note que lâ€™on pourrait Ã©galement trouver en abondance des cygnes et des canards. Ainsi, derriÃ¨re la description hydrographique dâ€™un passage difficile, encombrÃ© de bancs et de hauts-fonds, apparaÃ®t  lâ€™image dâ€™un milieu littoral frÃ©quentÃ© par de nombreux oiseaux dâ€™eau et de mer.
history:
As he approached the Entrance of the Casuarina, Freycinet at first thought he recognised the mouth of a river. This initial impression, however, soon gave way to a more cautious reading of the site: access was difficult, barred by numerous sandbanks, several of which dried at low tide. As he proceeded farther into the inlet from the east, the sandbanks became increasingly numerous, eventually closing off all passage.
The coastal landscape showed a clear contrast between the two shores. To the north, the low, damp ground was occupied by marshes. To the south, the coast rose into regular hills of moderate height, extending eastward as far as Circular Head without any marked indentations. Freycinet considered these lands to be of limited fertility, dominated by sandy soils, though nevertheless fairly well covered with vegetation. From a nautical perspective, the chief value of the place lay at its mouth: despite the shoals that made access difficult, it could offer small vessels a safe and well-sheltered anchorage, protected from winds from every direction.
Freycinet, whose attention remained primarily directed toward the nautical geography of the site, nevertheless also noted the richness of its birdlife. Around the Hunter Islands, he recorded the considerable presence of petrels and cormorants on the neighbouring islets. Within the Entrance of the Casuarina itself, he observed that swans and ducks could also be found in abundance. Thus, beyond the hydrographic description of a difficult passage, encumbered by banks and shoals, there emerges the image of a coastal environment frequented by numerous waterbirds and seabirds.

history: PÃ©ron recounts the navigational difficulties encountered by his companions during their reconnaissance of the Hunter Islands. He writes: â€œOur companions were already congratulating themselves on this important discovery [Boullanger Bay], when, on approaching Cape Berthoud, which forms the western point of the bay, the sounding suddenly fell from 10 to 3 fathoms and forced them to tack in order to escapeâ€¦ the winds blew with such violence that M. Freycinet, in order to relieve his vessel, which laboured heavily and was taking in as much as 10 inches of water an hour, was obliged to heave toâ€¦â€ The passage is a reminder of how exposed the survey of these coasts remained to the dangers of shoals, violent winds, and difficult seas. Pirates Bay is an embayed sandy shoreline, supplied by inherited and reworked quartz-rich sands and framed by resistant rocky coasts. Its geomorphological distinctiveness lies especially in the Tessellated Pavement: an intertidal platform formed in Permian rocks, where joints, marine abrasion, salt, and repeated wetting and drying cycles produce an exceptional natural pavement. More than a geological curiosity, this site offers a particularly clear expression of the dynamics of temperate rocky coasts exposed to the swells of the Tasman Sea.
The French named it after Gaspard Monge (1746-1818), the founding father of descriptive geometry that represents three-dimensional objects in two dimensions.

history:
Writing about the bay, PÃ©ron explains: â€œWe named it Monge Bay, in honour of the illustrious scholar to whom the physical and mathematical sciences owe so many valuable discoveries.â€
During his reconnaissance of the Tasman Peninsula and the shores of Monge Bay, the geographer Faure collected several rock samples. These were later described by the mineralogist Bailly in his "Catalogue of Mineral Specimens Collected during the Voyage of Discovery Commanded by Captain Baudin".

The â€œsiliceous stoneâ€ with a conchoidal fracture, apparently layered and elongated hexagonal in shape, can be related to the fine-grained, jointed and stratified rocks that form the coastal platforms of Pirates Bay. The â€œrounded blockâ€ of granite, meanwhile, bears witness to the mechanical reworking of rocks by wave action.
In this way, the stones described by Bailly are more than mineralogical specimens. They offer a concrete way of understanding how the rocky shores of the Tasman Peninsula and Monge Bay, now known as Pirates Bay, are formed and transformed by waves, salt and time.

history:
On 23 January 1802, in the dâ€™Entrecasteaux Channel, Baudin sent the officer Ronsard, accompanied by the young Bougainville, ashore to take on fresh water. For Baudin, the supply of fresh water was a vital matter for the continuation of the expedition, on which depended the health of the crew, the safety of the voyage, and the vesselsâ€™ ability to continue their coastal reconnaissance.

Bailly, the mineralogist, and the midshipman Charles Moreau (1781-1808) were assigned to the party, and both played an active role during the mission. After encountering great difficulties in crossing the bar at the mouth of the river and further upstream, they advanced probably as far as Whiteman Bridge at the eastern edge of Perth. Baillyâ€™s hope of reaching the hills they could see in the distance â€“ the Darling Ranges â€“ was not fulfilled, as the shortage of supplies made a speedy return necessary. Bailly was able to survey and describe the geology along the banks of the river and record observations of the fauna and flora. Moreau was given command of the long boat when Heirisson went exploring the shores.

characteristic:
Heirisson Island is a riverine island and protected natural reserve situated within the Swan River in Western Australia. Located between the suburbs of East Perth and Victoria Park, the island is notable for its population of free-ranging western grey kangaroos. It constitutes a site of cultural significance for the Noongar people, reflecting the long-standing Indigenous relationships with the riverine landscape.

This name thus placed the island group off the present-day Perth region within the political and dynastic nomenclature of the Consulate and, later, the Empire. Il prÃ©cise que ces Ã®les, ainsi que les rochers dissÃ©minÃ©s Ã  faible distance du continent, sont reliÃ©s par un banc de roches sâ€™Ã©tendant jusquâ€™Ã  prÃ¨s de trois lieues de la grande terre.

En revanche, ils ne trouvÃ¨rent nulle part de coquillages propres Ã  la consommation.
Freycinet ajoute que les Ã®les Louis-NapolÃ©on ne leur parurent pas habitÃ©es et quâ€™il ne semblait pas que les habitants de la terre ferme y fussent descendus. Cette remarque doit cependant Ãªtre comprise comme une observation europÃ©enne ponctuelle, faite au moment du passage de lâ€™expÃ©dition. Les connaissances actuelles invitent Ã  la nuancer : Wadjemup / Rottnest Island, Garden Island / Meeandip et Carnac Island / Ngooloormayup appartiennent au pays culturel whadjuk noongar. Rottnest, en particulier, fut reliÃ©e au continent avant la montÃ©e du niveau marin et frÃ©quentÃ©e par les Noongar avant son isolement ; aprÃ¨s cette sÃ©paration, lâ€™Ã®le conserva une forte signification spirituelle.
history:
Freycinet places the Louis-NapolÃ©on Islands within the broader setting of Terre dâ€™Ã‰dels, whose shores he describes as steep and difficult to approach, and which also includes the Swan River. In his account, the group consists of three principal islands: Rottnest, Buache â€” now Garden Island â€” and Bertholet â€” now Carnac Island. He adds that these islands, together with the rocks scattered a short distance from the mainland, are connected by a rocky bank extending nearly three leagues from the shore.
The narrative also emphasises both the richness and the dangers of the surrounding marine environment. Freycinet was especially struck by the abundance of sharks, which scarcely left the ship and several of which were "truly enormous". The fish considered good to eat included a kind of whiting, trevallies, and above all a hump-backed red fish, which the French associated with the dolphinfish family and whose flesh they found "particularly delicate". By contrast, they found no shellfish suitable for eating.
Freycinet further notes that the Louis-NapolÃ©on Islands did not appear to them to be inhabited, and that it did not seem that the people of the mainland had landed there. This remark, however, should be understood as a limited European observation made at the time of the expeditionâ€™s passage. Present-day knowledge invites a more nuanced view: Wadjemup / Rottnest Island, Garden Island / Meeandip and Carnac Island / Ngooloormayup all belong to Whadjuk Noongar Country. Rottnest, in particular, was once connected to the mainland before rising sea levels separated it, and it was visited by Noongar people before its isolation. After that separation, the island retained a strong spiritual significance.

It forms part of the Fisherman Islands Nature Reserve within the Jurien Bay Marine Park, a protected seascape distinguished by extensive seagrass meadows, rocky shorelines, sheltered sandy bays, and a high diversity of marine life.

The island takes its name from the senior geographer associated with the expedition that first charted the area.

characteristic_fr:
Le piton Lesueur, en Australie-Occidentale, est une mesa (montagne tabulaire) remarquable Ã  sommet plat, situÃ©e au sein du parc national de Lesueur et appartenant Ã  la chaÃ®ne de Gairdner. Sâ€™Ã©levant abruptement au-dessus dâ€™une ancienne plaine latÃ©ritique, sa stratigraphie met en Ã©vidence une succession de grÃ¨s et de quartzites anciens, surmontÃ©s dâ€™une duricroÃ»te rÃ©sistante, une structure qui a permis la conservation de la mesa par Ã©rosion diffÃ©rentielle tout en engendrant de forts escarpements et des falaises abruptes sur ses marges. Ces conditions gÃ©ologiques ont favorisÃ© la formation dâ€™une mosaÃ¯que dâ€™habitats et contribuÃ© Ã  des niveaux dâ€™endÃ©misme particuliÃ¨rement Ã©levÃ©s, notamment au sein dâ€™une flore riche en espÃ¨ces de fleurs sauvages, abondantes selon les saisons.

characteristic:
Mount Lesueur, Western Australia, is a prominent flat-topped mesa (tabletop mountain) located within Lesueur National Park and forming part of the Gairdner Range. Rising abruptly from an ancient lateritic plain, its stratigraphy reveals a sequence of ancient sandstones and quartzites capped by a resistant duricrust, a structure that has preserved the mesa through differential erosion while generating steep escarpments and sheer cliffs along its margins. These geological conditions have fostered a mosaic of habitats and contributed to the areaâ€™s exceptional levels of endemism, particularly among its diverse and seasonally abundant wildflower flora.
The toponym of the mount commemorates Charles-Alexandre Lesueur (1778â€“1846), who was appointed official artist of the Baudin expedition following its stopover at ÃŽle de France (Mauritius) in Marchâ€“April 1801 and the withdrawal of the artists Jacques-GÃ©rard Milbert and Michel Garnier initially designated by the commission responsible for selecting the expeditionâ€™s scientists.
history_fr: The Pitons (peaks) Lesueur and PÃ©ron are low, flat-topped hills located near the coast to the north of Perth, in Lesueur National Park. Their proximity in the local landscape reflects the friendship and close working relationship of Charles-Alexandre Lesueur (1778-1846) and FranÃ§ois PÃ©ron (see the entry Canal PÃ©ron, on Tasmania). PÃ©ron addressed Lesueur in letters as â€˜Mon cher papa Lesueurâ€™, despite being three years older, and, at the time of his death, bequeathed to him his manuscripts of the voyage. PÃ©ron states that â€˜My friend M Lesueur was always my collaborator and companion; what I described with care, he drew or painted it with the accuracy and skill that deserve much praise.â€™ (â€˜Mon ami M. Lesueur fut toujours mon collaborateur et mon compagnon ; ce que je m'appliquois Ã  dÃ©crire avec soin, il le dessinoit ou le peignoit lui-mÃªme avec cette exactitude et cette habiletÃ© qui lui ont mÃ©ritÃ© tant d'honorables suffragesâ€™.) They took it in turns to make continuous observations and to collect marine life when under sail onboard le GÃ©ographe. Together, they itemised a rich collection of all classes of animals including new species as well as some living species that were known only as fossils in Europe
history:
In a report on the voyage presented to the government by a committee of the Institut on 9 June 1806, its chairman, Georges Cuvier, emphasised the scale and scientific significance of the collections deposited at the MusÃ©um by PÃ©ron and Lesueur. He noted that they comprised â€œmore than one hundred thousand animal specimens, among which several new genera were discovered; that the number of new species exceeds 2,500; and that Messrs PÃ©ron and Lesueur alone have made known more animals than all the travelling naturalists of recent times.â€ Cuvier further observed that M. Lesueur, who assisted PÃ©ron in collecting these specimens,was able, through his drawings, to provide â€œthe most accurate information on their modes of life and their habitats.â€
Beyond this institutional recognition, the relationship between Lesueur and PÃ©ron was characterised by close scientific collaboration and deep personal affinity. Companions in the field as well as intellectual partners, they worked jointly in observation, collection, and description, to such an extent that Lesueur was sometimes regarded, beyond his role as an artist, as a zoologist in his own right. 
Before assuming the positions of the expeditionâ€™s official artists alongside his colleague Nicolas Petit, both men had embarked on the voyage as assistant gunners. Baudin was well aware of their abilities as draftsmen and favoured their embarkation precisely for this reason.

characteristic:
At the most northerly point of Dirk Hartog Island,Cape Inscription is the site of the earliest known landings of Europeans on the western coast of the Australian continent. The first known European landing on the west coast of Australia was by Dirk Hartog of the Dutch East India Company's ship the Eendracht at Cape Inscription on 25 October 1616.  Hartog left a pewter plate, inscribed with a record of his visit and nailed to a post left standing upright in a rock cleft on top of the cliff. Dirk Hartog's plate was recovered by Willem de Vlamingh during his voyage of discovery in 1697.  He in turn left a pewter plate inscribed with a record of his visit.  The plate left by Vlamingh was in turn found in 1801 by Hamelin's quartermaster/chief yeoman, Le Thiers,that Hamelin sent in mission in Dirk Hartog island on the 20-21 July 1801 Hemelin left the plate in place and added his own inscription on a piece of lead sheet, nailed to another post.
It was in reference to this succession of inscriptions that the French expedition subsequently designated the site Cap de lâ€™Inscription.

Toutefois, Hamelin opta pour une dÃ©signation dâ€™une portÃ©e plus historique et mÃ©morielle en donnant au cap un nom faisant Ã©cho Ã  la succession des commandants qui, au fil des voyages, y avaient inscrit la trace de leur passage au moyen dâ€™inscriptions gravÃ©es sur des plaques dressÃ©es au sommet du cap.

On the earliest chart drawn by Faure aboard the Naturaliste, the toponym initially proposed was â€œPointe du Retourâ€ (â€œReturn Pointâ€), intended to mark the fact that, during their reconnaissance of the Bay of Chiens-Marins, Freycinet and Faure reached this northernmost point before turning back toward the corvette. Hamelin, however, opted for a designation of broader historical and memorial significance, bestowing upon the cape a name that alluded to the succession of commanders who, over the course of successive voyages, had inscribed the trace of their passage there by means of engraved inscriptions on plaques erected at the summit of the cape.

history:
Hamelin sets out the routes to be followed when entering Dampier Bay through the Naturaliste Passage. He distinguishes between two options, passing either north or south of Dampier Reef, and draws navigatorsâ€™ attention to the shoals near Dirk Hartog Island, which are all the more dangerous because they do not always break.
Freycinet, for his part, emphasises the quality of the anchorage within the bay. He notes that when the ships left Dampier Bay in August 1801, the sea became â€œextremely roughâ€ as soon as they entered the Naturaliste Passage, whereas at their anchorage it had previously been only slightly swell-marked. Despite a fresh breeze and the topgallant masts having remained struck up, the ships had not dragged their anchors â€œby an inchâ€

history: On 18 July 1801, Hamelin sent Saint-Cricq to the southern part of Dorre Island. He was asked  to check for the presence of reefs, for suitable beaches to land, to make observations on tides and on the areaâ€™s natural history, to erect a French flag and leave a bottle on the beach containing a note to attest to the visit of le Naturaliste, and to check for signs of an earlier landing on the island by crew members of le GÃ©ographe. Saint Cricq was impressed by the rock-strewn nature of the island that he described as â€˜an enormous mass of rocks piled up one on top of the other in very regular layersâ€™. In recognition of his work, the southern cape of the island was named after him. (See also Baie de lâ€™Attaque).

Exhausted by his march, he wrote: â€œOverwhelmed by fatigue and drenched in sweat, I fell to the ground; and, quite unable to continue on my way, I resolved to spend the rest of the night there, even if I were to perish in the midst of this dreadful desertâ€¦â€ His temporary disappearance caused the commander such concern that he chose not to reproach him for the imprudence of his actions.

But while he was trying to detach them from the rock, â€œa violent wave broke with such force over the crest of the reefâ€ that he was thrown against the neighbouring rocks and rolled across the reefs. His clothes were torn to pieces, and he found himself â€œcovered with wounds, drenched in bloodâ€.
It was Freycinet who gave the island the name Bernier, in honour of the expeditionâ€™s astronomer, who â€œhad fixed its position through numerous observationsâ€. Bernier had worked there to â€œdetermine the latitude and longitude and to fix precisely the entrance to Shark Bayâ€. history: Le GÃ©ographe crossed the passage on 27 and 28 June 1801, then anchored to the south of it, off Bernier Island. Freycinet provides a precise nautical description of the passage: between Cape Cuvier and Koks Islet lies a broad strait, free from any kind of danger, which the expeditionâ€™s charts named Passage du GÃ©ographe. Approximately ten leagues wide along a north-north-east / south-south-west axis, it formed one of the two main entrances to Shark Bay, the other being the Passage du Naturaliste. characteristic:
Situated to the west of the Peron Peninsula, Henri Freycinet Harbour, also known as Freycinet Estuary, is one of the inner gulfs of Shark Bay. The harbour is characterised by a higher density of islands than the neighbouring Hamelin Pool and, in its northern sector, by a series of narrow peninsulas locally known as 'prongs'.
Incorporated within the Shark Bay Marine Park and included in the Shark Bay World Heritage Area, the estuary has been identified as an important habitat for dugongs and forms part of a broader marine environment noted for its ecological specificity, including the presence of stromatolites. At the same time, the harbour lies within a zone that has undergone industrial modification, notably through dredging associated with the Shark Bay Salt Joint Venture.
The harbour was named by Louis de Freycinet in honour of his elder brother Henri (see also Presquâ€™Ã®le Freycinet, Tasmania).

history:
Freycinet reports that sandbanks appear almost entirely to obstruct the entrance to the harbour, whose indentation extends along a south-eastâ€“north-west axis. He estimates its length at twenty-two leagues (approximately 120 km) and its width at two to three leagues (just over 15 km). Although the harbour is not entirely free of banks, he notes that its central basin nevertheless includes numerous areas of considerable depth, while shoals occur continuously along the margins. Navigation is therefore often possible in the deeper central waters, but remains hazardous close to the shores. Freycinet also notes the presence of salt ponds along the shoreline north of the Peron Peninsula.
PÃ©ron, by contrast, dwells at length on the presence of dugongs in his descriptions of both Henri Freycinet Harbour and Hamelin Harbour. In explaining the erroneous name â€œShark Bayâ€ given by Dampier, he observes that the English navigator had only a head partially decomposed by digestion before him. He further notes that the two elongated teeth mentioned by Dampierâ€”an additional source of confusionâ€”are found in both the hippopotamus and the dugong, differing only in their position: in the former they are borne by the lower jaw, in the latter by the upper.

While participating in the practices of specimen collection typical of natural history at the time, including the killing of animals for study, Leschenault was nonetheless sensitised to the harsh and relentless slaughter carried out by the expeditionâ€™s hunters, who killed kangaroos in Shark Bayâ€”animals that, as he observed, â€œhave no enemies to fear other than a few sea eagles; by taking refuge beneath impenetrable bushes, they defy the voracity of these birds. We came and brought strife and terror to their refugeâ€¦â€.
He further recounts that â€œone of the young kangaroos, which was slower than the others in leaving its place of shelter, was captured; and instead of appearing frightened, it licked the man who had just killed its mother and accepted the food offered to it. The Commander kept the animal for several months, until it died accidentally at Timor. It became perfectly tame, affectionate, and easy to feed. It ate bread and was particularly fond of sugar water.â€

history:
Peron recounts that during his excursion on the east coast (19 march 1803) of the Peron Peninsula in the company of Guichenault and Petit, while they were wading in the water to collect beautiful specimens of shells, a shark suddenly approached Petit. Peron writes: "Petit, in his fear, fired at the animal. Not doubting that the savages, whose recent tracks we had just seen on the shore, would hurry back at the sound, I suggested to my companions that we leave the sea immediately to retrieve our clothes and hide in the bushes. But the imprudent Mr Petit insisted, despite our pleas, on remaining in the sea, even mocking our caution. His indiscreet confidence soon gave way to terror. Indeed... we saw a group of savages who, from the top of a large dune (Cap Guichenault), were rushing towards the shore. At this sight, even Mr Petit, filled with terror, hastily grabbed some of his clothes and ran half-naked to join us..."
Petit died a few months after his return from a bad fall that resulted in a gangrene.

PÃ©ron recalls, in a footnote concerning Baie des Chiens-Marins â€” present-day Shark Bay â€” that turtles were particularly abundant on Faure Island during the Naturalisteâ€™s first stay in August 1801. Freycinet describes Cape Cuvier as a landform rising prominently above the surrounding terrain, appearing like â€œan enormous bastionâ€ and, like many points along this coast, distinguished by a particularly vivid red hue, visible from afar, although â€œno trace of vegetation can be perceived there.â€ He further notes that the position of the cape was established on the basis of the observations and surveys carried out by the geographer Boullanger aboard the GÃ©ographe.

The mollusc specimens collected there provided Georges Cuvier with privileged empirical material in support of his conception of the order of the living world. The study of these organisms enabled him to demonstrate that, despite the diversity of external forms, constant anatomical structuresâ€”such as shells, nervous systems, and the organisation of organsâ€”make possible a rigorous classification of species and a clear distinction between major zoological groups. For Cuvier, this anatomical stability constituted evidence of the immutability of species and of the existence of a fixed natural order, a conception that PÃ©ron himself did not necessarily share without reservation.

The latter, more dangerous to approach than any of the previous ones, is dotted with a multitude of islands and islets, shoals and reefs, which often hinder navigation..."

Born into a modest background, Claude-Victor Perrin began his career in the artillery as a private soldier â€” some biographies even state that he first entered the army as a drummer â€” before rising through the ranks to the marshalate. His career thus makes him a representative figure of the military advancement opened up by the Revolution.
In 1796, he commanded a brigade in Italy within the division under Augereau. He fought at Dego before joining MassÃ©naâ€™s division. Victor then took possession of the town of Peschiera, took part in the actions at Roveredo and Cerea, and was wounded at Saint-Georges.

Promoted to general of division, he subsequently fought the troops of the Papal army. In 1800, sent to reinforce General Lannes at Montebello, he helped contain the Austrian attack at Marengo until Lannes arrived to support him.

history:
Freycinet writes of the group of islands he named Montebello that â€œthe great distance at which our vessels passed them allowed us to see them only very imperfectlyâ€. He distinguished only three islands, those shown on the map in the voyage atlas and corresponding today to Hermite Island, Trimouille Island and Lowendal Island.
This first reconnaissance was therefore highly incomplete. Modern geographical knowledge shows that the Montebello â€” or Monte Bello â€” Islands in fact form a large archipelago of about 174 islands and islets, only around thirty of which are named, lying some 130 kilometres off the Pilbara coast. Modern cartography also distinguishes the Lowendal Islands as a separate group farther south, while the Montebello archipelago proper lies farther north.
Yet this initial representation, however imperfect geographically, is especially significant symbolically. The names associated with this sector trace a French military memory extending across a long span of history, from the Italian Wars of the Renaissance to the conflicts of the Revolutionary and Napoleonic periods. La Trimouille refers to Louis II de La TrÃ©moille, a figure of the Italian Wars who died at Pavia in 1525; Lowendal evokes Ulrich Frederick Woldemar de Lowendal, Marshal of France in the eighteenth century and victor at Bergen op Zoom during the War of the Austrian Succession; Hermite recalls Jean-Marthe-Adrien Lâ€™Hermite, a naval officer who took part in the Battle of Prairial in 1794, later distinguished himself in the Indian Ocean, and in 1805-1806 led a successful raiding campaign against British shipping in the Atlantic; Montebello, finally, refers to the French victory of 1800 in northern Italy, associated with General Lannes*.
The juxtaposition of these names therefore shows how French nomenclature projected onto the geography of the Southern Lands a national memory that was not limited to the immediate Napoleonic present. It drew on military episodes ranging from the sixteenth century to the Consulate and Empire, through the wars of the eighteenth century and the naval battles of the Revolution.

In the 1950s, three British nuclear tests were conducted on the Montebello Islands. Today, the islands form part of a marine conservation reserve, located only a few kilometres from major gas fields and pipeline corridors, illustrating the juxtaposition of environmental protection and industrial activity in the region.
The islands take their name from Jean Lannes (see the entry Cap Lannes in South Australia), one of Napoleonâ€™s most distinguished generals, who was created Duke of Montebello in recognition of his role in securing victory at the Battle of Montebello (Province of Pavia, Lombardy) against Austrian forces.

history:
Freycinet writes of the group of islands he named Montebello that â€œthe great distance at which our vessels passed them allowed us to see them only very imperfectlyâ€. He distinguished only three islands, those shown on the map in the voyage atlas and corresponding today to Hermite Island, Trimouille Island and Lowendal Island.
This first reconnaissance was therefore highly incomplete. Modern geographical knowledge shows that the Montebello â€” or Monte Bello â€” Islands in fact form a large archipelago of about 174 islands and islets, only around thirty of which are named, lying some 130 kilometres off the Pilbara coast. Modern cartography also distinguishes the Lowendal Islands as a separate group farther south, while the Montebello archipelago proper lies farther north.
Yet this initial representation, however imperfect geographically, is especially significant symbolically. The names associated with this sector trace a French military memory extending across a long span of history, from the Italian Wars of the Renaissance to the conflicts of the Revolutionary and Napoleonic periods. La Trimouille refers to Louis II de La TrÃ©moille, a figure of the Italian Wars who died at Pavia in 1525; Lowendal evokes Ulrich Frederick Woldemar de Lowendal, Marshal of France in the eighteenth century and victor at Bergen op Zoom during the War of the Austrian Succession; Hermite recalls Jean-Marthe-Adrien Lâ€™Hermite, a naval officer who took part in the Battle of Prairial in 1794, later distinguished himself in the Indian Ocean, and in 1805-1806 led a successful raiding campaign against British shipping in the Atlantic; Montebello, finally, refers to the French victory of 1800 in northern Italy, associated with General Lannes*.
The juxtaposition of these names therefore shows how French nomenclature projected onto the geography of the Southern Lands a national memory that was not limited to the immediate Napoleonic present. It drew on military episodes ranging from the sixteenth century to the Consulate and Empire, through the wars of the eighteenth century and the naval battles of the Revolution.

Trimouille Island is known as the site of Operation Hurricane, the first British nuclear test, which took place in a bay on the island in 1952. Two further nuclear tests were conducted in 1956, the second again on Trimouille Island. This test, code-named Mosaic G2, was the largest nuclear explosion ever carried out in Australia, with a yield of approximately 98 kilotonnes.
The island takes its name from Louis II de La TrÃ©moille (1460â€“1525), also known as La Trimouille, a French general descended from an ancient noble family of Brittany. He served successively under three French kingsâ€”Charles VIII, Louis XII, and Francis Iâ€”and was killed in combat in Lombardy while protecting Francis I during the Battle of Pavia in 1525.

characteristic:
Hermite Island is the largest island in the Montebello group, which consists mainly of low-lying limestone islands.
It is named after Jean-Marthe-Adrien Lâ€™Hermite (1766â€“1826), a French naval officer who later rose to the rank of rear admiral. Lâ€™Hermite took part in the Fourth Battle of Ushant, also known as the Glorious First of June, or, in French, the combat de Prairial, fought between the French and British navies off the island of Ushant in the Atlantic Ocean from 28 May to 1 June 1794. He was then serving aboard the flagship of the Brest squadron, La Montagne, under the command of Villaret de Joyeuse.
Lâ€™Hermite also distinguished himself through his actions in the Indian Ocean. Bonaparte described him as â€œbraveâ€. In 1805â€“1806, at the head of his squadron, he captured numerous British ships in the Atlantic, a success that earned him promotion to rear admiral.

The Dampier Archipelago, located off the north-west coast of Western Australia, consists of a group of forty-two islands. Aboriginal populations have inhabited this region for approximately 50,000 years, and the archipelago is rich in archaeological sites, including rock art assemblages of remarkable scale and significance.
In the contemporary period, the presence of vast offshore gas and oil fields, together with nearby rich deposits of iron ore, has driven large-scale industrial development. This dynamic has transformed the port of Dampier into a major hub for the export of Australiaâ€™s natural resources, resulting in a landscape in which industrial infrastructure and long-standing cultural heritage coexist in close proximity.
The French named the archipelago in honour of William Dampier, an English buccaneer, navigator and explorer who landed on this coast in 1699. This designation reflects the state of European knowledge at the time, based exclusively on earlier maritime accounts and unaware of the millennia-old Indigenous cultural heritage for which the region is now recognised as one of the most significant testimonies.

Depuch Island is a rocky island separated from the mainland coast by approximately 3 km of shallow waters and tidal mudflats bordered by mangroves. The island is largely composed of angular dolerite blocks, with only limited sandy beaches along its southern and eastern shores. Sparse soils occur between rock outcrops on the summit and within deeply incised valleys. Roughly oblong in shape, Depuch Island measures about 5 km in length and 3 km in width, rising to 160 meters (Depuch Beacon).
The island holds exceptional cultural significance as a major Ngarluma Aboriginal site, containing one of the highest concentrations of rock engravings in Australia, comparable to those of the Burrup Peninsula. Depuch Island was named after the expeditionâ€™s senior mineralogist Louis Depuch (1774â€“1803, see Anse Depuch, in WA).

De son cÃ´tÃ©, Freycinet note : Â« Dâ€™aprÃ¨s les observations de M. Ronsard, lâ€™Ã®le Depuch ne paraÃ®t pas habitÃ©e, Ã  moins que lâ€™on ne veuille considÃ©rer comme preuve dâ€™un sÃ©jour temporaire de lâ€™homme sur ces rivages la rencontre de deux troncs dâ€™arbres brÃ»lÃ©s et de quelques laves rÃ©cemment fracturÃ©es Â» (p. 231).

history:
Depuch confirmed that the rock specimens collected by Ronsard during his visit to the island on 27 July 1801, made in the GÃ©ographeâ€™s longboat, were basalt, and, together with Ronsard, inferred that the island was of volcanic origin.
PÃ©ron explained: â€œThe colour of this basalt,â€ says my interesting friend Depuch, who had several samples at his disposal, â€œis greyish blue; its texture is very dense, its grain fine and siliceous in appearance; small shiny blades, irregularly distributed, are scattered throughout the mass; it does not effervesce with acids and does not significantly affect the magnetic bar; its outer part has undergone a kind of alteration produced by ferruginous molecules â€¦â€.
For his part, Freycinet noted: â€œAccording to the observations of M. Ronsard, Depuch Island does not appear to be inhabited, unless, however, one wishes to consider as proof of manâ€™s temporary stay on these shores the encounter there of two burnt tree trunks and some freshly broken lavaâ€ (p. 231).
Today, geological studies identify the islandâ€™s rocks as doleriteâ€”an igneous rock with the same chemical composition as basalt but with larger crystal sizesâ€”and Depuch Island is recognised for its long-standing Aboriginal presence, with more than 5,000 individual rock engravings recorded.

history:
AndrÃ© Thouin (1746â€“1824) was an influential and well-connected French botanist. In 1793, he was appointed to the chair of horticulture at the MusÃ©um national d'histoire naturelle in Paris. 
In the context of the revolutionary wars, some men of science, including Thouin, accepted the universality of the revolutionary ideals and that the seizure of natural history collections and rare live animals as prizes of war was permissible. When, in 1794, the French revolutionary armies occupied the Netherlands, Thouin with BarthÃ©lemy Faujas de Saint-Fond (see Piton Faujas*, Tasmania) directed the move to Paris, of Dutch natural history collections, including life elephants and stuffed animals derived from countries overseas. Later, in 1796, he was charged with a similar mission, during Bonaparte's Italian campaign. 
In June 1803, when le Naturaliste returned to Le Havre with a large part of the Baudin expeditionâ€™s collections of natural history specimens, the senator Jean-Antoine Chaptal, a well-regarded scientist keen to develop in agriculture new methods such as the technique of adding sugar to unfermented grape to increase their alcohol level after fermentation (chaptalisation), sent Thouin to receive and take care of them. Unfortunately, most of the plant specimens collected in Australia did not survive the voyage, but some seeds from plants new to France were germinated, e.g., eucalypts and acacias. On their arrival in Paris, Thouin was ordered to send a large part of the collections, including kangaroos, cassowaries, tortoises and black swans, to the empressâ€™s garden at La Malmaison, rather than to the Museum and its menagerie.

history:
Jacques Bedout was a Canadian-born naval officer who spent fourteenth years in the merchant navy at the beginning of his career, before joining the French Navy. 
After the French disaster of Trafalgar in 1805, Bedout ended his official career with the painful task of presiding over the Council of War to judge the officers whose conduct had been deemed reprehensible during the attack on the fleet on the Isle of Aix by English fire ships. Of the four officers involved, two were sentenced, one to death.

history:
Missiessy was a French naval officer promoted to the rank of rear admiral in 1793. Like many officers during the Terror, he temporarily left France before returning to service. On his return, he was entrusted by Jean-Charles de Borda with assisting in the introduction of the metric system in French ports and naval arsenals, and he played a key role in the standardisation of coastal signals, contributing to safer navigation and improved maritime communication.
Between 1804 and 1805, Missiessy commanded the Rochefort squadron during British blockades in the West Indies, successfully supplying French colonies and disrupting British operations without losing a ship. His mission formed part of a broader diversionary strategy linked to Napoleonâ€™s planned invasion of England, though the lack of full strategic disclosure limited coordination with other French squadrons.
Missiessy was also recognised for his influential treatise on the fitting-out and internal organisation of warships, published in 1797. Drawing on the model of a 74-gun vessel, the work promoted standardised layouts, clarified the functions of shipboard spaces, defined precise roles for crew members, and proposed technical improvements to enhance manoeuvrability and combat readiness. 
For all these reasons, Missiessy appears to have been held in high esteem by Freycinet.

history: Pierre-Joseph Desault was a surgeon who introduced an innovative method of teaching anatomy based on the systematic study of human corpses. As chief surgeon at the HÃ´tel-Dieu in Paris, he transformed surgical training by combining lectures and close supervision with live clinical practice, teaching while performing surgical procedures. Desault trained many of the most renowned surgeons of his time, notably Jean-Nicolas Corvisart, Pierre-FranÃ§ois Percy, and Dominique Jean Larrey. The political violence and radicalism of the Terror took a severe toll on his own health, contributing to his premature death.

Louis-Claude de Saulces de Freycinet notes: â€œNear the sea, the ground consists of white sand dunes and cliffs of vividly coloured red rock; a continuous reef, awash at the surface, extends along most of the coastline, though without projecting far offshore. It is at the point where the reef begins to the south that the reddish cliffs we have just mentioned also commence; they extend uninterrupted as far as Coulomb Point, beyond which the dunes reappear.
A ravine observed along the coast, between Cape Boileau and Coulomb Point, appeared to us to be the bed of a stream; the greater freshness of the vegetation at this location seems to support this conjectureâ€ (p. 239).

This description corresponds closely to the present-day morphology of Coulomb Point, which is primarily composed of sandstone whose characteristic red colour results from iron oxides formed through the weathering and oxidation of iron-bearing minerals within the rock matrix.

characteristic:
Cape Bertholet corresponds to a low coastal headland. At this point, the coastline shifts orientation and trends eastward, forming a 14 km-long stretch of very low-energy shore that extends into the extensive tidal flats of Carnot Bay. This sector is characterised by a gently sloping coastal profile and limited wave action.
Between the cape and the bay extends a low-lying barrier system, bordered by mangrove forests at both ends and enclosing central salt flats reaching up to 3 km in width.

history:
The proximity of Coulomb Point and Cape Bertholet within the nomenclature is unlikely to be coincidental. Rather, it may reflect the logic of institutional affiliation that underpinned the naming practices of the expedition, whereby Claude Louis Berthollet succeeded Charles-Augustin de Coulomb as president of the Class of Mathematical and Physical Sciences at the Institut National des Sciences et des Arts in 1801. The spatial association of their names along the same stretch of coast thus mirrors their succession within the scientific hierarchy of the Institut.

history:
The attribution of this toponym forms part of a system of naming closely connected to the scholarly networks of the Institut National des Sciences et des Arts (now the Institut de France), of which Lazare Nicolas Marguerite Carnot was one of the leading figures.
Like Charles-Augustin de Coulomb, Carnot applied mathematics to the study of physics. His work in geometry proved particularly valuable to engineer-geographersâ€”especially those of the Baudin expeditionâ€”as well as to naval officers such as Louis-Claude de Saulces de Freycinet, insofar as it went beyond Euclidean geometryâ€”centred on the study of shapes and figuresâ€”to develop more general approaches and demonstrations that could be applied in surveying and field measurement operations.

The reefs proved hazardous to the Casuarina, commanded by Louis-Claude de Saulces de Freycinet, when, on 10 April 1803, it was carried by the currents into a tide race near these shoals.

characteristic:
The Bonaparte Archipelago is located in the Kimberley region of Western Australia, within the Countries of the Balanggarra, Wunambal Gaambera, Ngarinyin, and Miriuwung Gajerrong peoples.
The archipelago comprises several hundred islands and islets lying offshore along the stretch of coast between Collier Bay to the southwest and Admiralty Gulf to the northeast, including islands within Admiralty Gulf itself. Most are small islands, islets, or emergent rocks, and the area also includes several submerged banks and shoals.
Its coastal and marine habitats are characterised by reefs, mangroves, seagrass meadows, and intertidal mud and sand flats. A total of 225 species of hard corals have been recorded in the intertidal zone. The region is subject to large tidal fluctuations, with tidal ranges of around 8 metres, and to high turbidity. It is also influenced by broader-scale currents, including the Indonesian Throughflow via the inner North-West Shelf. The area is recognised as an Important Shark and Ray Area, supporting threatened species as well as key reproductive habitats.

The name thus reflects the political context in which Bonaparte sponsored the voyage of discovery to the Southern Lands (see also Golfe Bonaparte* in South Australia).

history:
Freycinet warns: â€œThe number of reefs that extend throughout the Bonaparte Archipelago, together with the considerable number of islands and islets found there, makes navigation in these waters difficult and even dangerous. As all the reefs are awash, the sounding line cannot indicate their proximity; this would be a very serious inconvenience at night, were it not for the ease with which one can anchor almost everywhere, allowing the navigator each evening to place himself out of danger. In some places the reefs extend far offshore, whereas elsewhere they lie close to the coast. Our chart indicates a great many of them; however, one must not suppose that they have all been marked. Especially between the islands, we have reason to believe that there are many banks that we did not examine. Thus, until a complete survey of these waters has been carried out, it is advisable to proceed with the utmost caution.â€ (*Navigation et GÃ©ographie*, p. 248).
For his part, PÃ©ron states: â€œUnder the general designation of Bonaparte Archipelago (chart no. 27), we have included the long succession of islands and islets extending from the Champagny Islands in the west to the Institut Islands, over a distance of about 60 nautical leagues. This vast ensemble is made up of different groups, which have been designated by the names of Champagny Islands, Arcole Islands, Maret Islands, Montalivet Islands, and Institut Islandsâ€ (PÃ©ron, vol. I, p. 140). See the next entries.

The Champagny Islands are located off the coast of the Kimberley region, in the southern part of the Bonaparte Archipelago, and form part of the Camden Sound Marine Park, known to the Traditional Owners as Nimenba. Today, they are recognised for their rich bird biodiversity. The islands are also known turtle nesting sites (Green Turtles and Hawksbill Turtles, which are both listed as threatened or endangered) and are fringed by extensive intertidal reefs on both their seaward and landward sides, across a range of depths.

Baudin asked his artists to sketch in colour the â€œbizarre and picturesqueâ€ forms of these islands, which were later included in the historical atlas of the expedition. 
PÃ©ron even wrote that several of them resembled â€œimmense ancient tombs.â€ His narrative also shows that the expedition observed and documented the very large tidal ranges characteristic of the Kimberley waters, which are among the greatest in the world. He notes: â€œDuring the time we remained at anchor among the Dâ€™Arcole Islands, the difference in soundings showed us that the tide rises here by 20 to 25 feet; an observation that seems to confirm that of M. This long cycle of conflicts, interrupted by truces, opposed the Plantagenet kingdom of England and the Valois kingdom of France from 1337 to 1453. history: Bertrand du Guesclin is most commonly associated with the following saying: â€œCourage grants what beauty denies.â€ The phrase encapsulates the traditional image of a man reputed to be physically ungainly, yet whose bravery, endurance, and military abilities made him one of the great French heroes of the Hundred Yearsâ€™ War. Legend also relates that, after being taken prisoner in Spain by the Prince of Wales following the Battle of NÃ¡jera on 3 April 1367, and while the terms of his ransom were being discussed, he declared: â€œMy lord, the King of Castile will pay one half, and the King of France the rest; and if that should not suffice, there is not a spinner in France who can spin who would not earn my ransom by spinning.â€ The remark symbolically suggests the depth of support he enjoyed throughout the kingdom of France, even among its humblest people. King Charles V of France (1338-1380) appointed him Constable of France in 1370, thus making him commander of the royal armies. Napoleon admired in him both his martial tenacity, his methodical reconquest of territories held by the English, and his use of an attritional strategy akin to scorched-earth warfare. Yet history carries a certain irony, for the Emperor failed to foresee that the Russians would employ comparable methods against him during the campaign of 1812.

history:
Freycinet states that he relied on the observational tables established by the astronomer-mathematicians Bouguer and La Caille in order to determine the time at which high tide should occur at the moment of the new and full moon. He also mentions the method proposed by Bernoulli, though he considered it less convenient to use: â€œBernouilli has given one whose form is different and whose use is less convenient; perhaps it is more exact in theory, but in practice such extreme exactness is still far from being found in observations.â€
This remark is revealing of the importance Freycinet attached to the gap between the elegance of mathematical constructions and the concrete constraints of navigation, where the precision of calculation constantly had to contend with the uncertainty of measurements taken at sea.

characteristic:
This group of islands lies off the Kimberley coast, in the northern sector of the Bonaparte Archipelago, and now forms part of the North Kimberley Marine Park. It is notable for its rich marine environments: more than 210 species of hard coral have been recorded on the Maret Islands alone (Richards et al. 2015). The archipelago also includes a highly diverse mangrove system, together with important dugong hotspots extending from the Maret Islands to the Montalivet Islands.
The Maret Islands take their name from Hugues-Bernard Maret, Secretary of State (see Maret Bay in South Australia). The principal island of the group likewise bears the name Maret Island.

Maret was honoured by having a group of islands named after him because, in his capacity as Minister-Secretary of State, he stood at the very centre of the imperial regime. He served as the indispensable intermediary through whom passed discussions, correspondence, projects, as well as the work and petitions of the other ministers. Maret also appears to have exercised a significant influence over appointments. Freycinet notes that the Maret Islands are marked by steep, high red cliffs, with the exception of Jussieu Island, which he describes as sandy and wooded.

history: The name Guillaume-Tell [William Tell] in the nomenclature invokes the figure of the patriotic hero who, in tradition, fought for the independence of his native Switzerland against Habsburg authority in the fourteenth century. The legend of William Tell, which became an important element of Swiss national memory, was revived during the French Revolution and reinterpreted as an emblem of resistance to tyranny, particularly that associated with the dynastic monarchies of Europe. This political and moral appropriation also found expression in music, when AndrÃ© GrÃ©try composed a comic opera on the subject in Paris in 1791, a work much admired by NapolÃ©on. During the revolutionary period, an eighty-gun ship of the French Navy likewise bore the name Guillaume Tell. Captured by the British in 1800 after the Battle of the Nile, during the Egyptian Campaign (see glossary), the vessel further illustrates the circulation of this heroic name within the military and political culture of the age.

By its inclusion in this nomenclature, Condillac appears among those leading intellectual authorities whose works continued to shape the learned and political culture of the Napoleonic period.

His presence in this nomenclature recalls the enduring importance of his work within the learned culture that the expedition sought to honour.
Pascal observed that, in his own age â€” and the reflection remained pertinent in the Revolutionary and Imperial periods, as it still does today â€” falsehood, illusion, private interests, social habits, and dominant forms of discourse can so fill the public sphere that truth becomes difficult to discern. It is not absent, in his view, so much as obscured, covered over by noise, manipulation, pride, or fear. He thus suggested that truth can only truly be recognised by those prepared to prefer it to their comfort, their prejudices, and sometimes even their advantage. Hence his often-quoted warning: â€œTruth is so obscured in these times, and falsehood so established, that unless one loves truth, one cannot know it.â€

history: PÃ©ron gave the name of Corneille to one of the Institut Islands, the Institut itself appearing as a kind of living encyclopaedia of French culture. Pierre Corneille naturally had his place there as one of the greatest writers of French classicism. He profoundly renewed tragedy by making it a theatre of action, decision, and moral greatness, at a time when French tragedy still often remained marked by imitation of antiquity, long speeches, and relatively static action. By placing heroic will at the centre of the stage, he gave tragedy an energy and dignity that were well suited to appeal to Bonaparte, who was particularly responsive to this dramaturgy of honour, command, and self-mastery.

history: Montesquieu remained well known to the cultivated elites and savants of the Napoleonic period, especially those connected with the Institut national des sciences et des arts (Institut de France), as a thinker of law, education, Roman history, and the moderation of governments. His works continued to circulate through new editions published by Didot in Year XII (1803) and by Billois in Year XIII (1805).
